svg.text: BRITISH SIGN LANGUAGE
HISTORY — EUROPEAN DEAF EDUCATION
BSL ORIGINS
BSL: emerged from Deaf communities in England; no clear founding date
1760: Thomas Braidwood opens first school for the Deaf in Edinburgh
1783: Braidwood Academy moves to London; oral methods emphasis
BSL community preserved sign language despite oralist educational pressure
MILAN CONFERENCE 1880: GLOBAL SUPPRESSION
1880: Second International Congress of Educators of the Deaf, Milan
Vote (mostly hearing educators): sign languages banned from schools
Oralism mandated: Deaf children must learn to speak, lip-read only
~100 years: BSL, DGS, ASL all suppressed in formal education
BSL RECOGNITION AND REVIVAL
1970s: British Deaf Association advocates for BSL recognition
1992: first BSL TV programs; 2003: UK government recognition
2022: BSL Act — BSL statutory protected language in England, Wales, Scotland
DGS HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
DGS ORIGINS
Germany: first Deaf school founded 1778 in Leipzig by Samuel Heinicke
Heinicke: oral method proponent; DGS developed despite oralist pressure
Hamburg: major Deaf community center; DGS standardized around Hamburg
DGS AND NAZI PERSECUTION
1933-1945: Nazi regime sterilized Deaf Germans under eugenics laws
Estimated 17,000 Deaf Germans forcibly sterilized; some sent to camps
DGS survived; community rebuilt slowly after WWII
DGS RECOGNITION
2002: DGS recognized in German Disability Equality Act
~200,000 DGS users; Hamburg University: major DGS research center
BSL AND DGS: SHARED EUROPEAN STRUGGLE
Both: suppressed by 1880 Milan Conference for ~100 years
Both: survived in Deaf communities despite educational bans
Both: officially recognized early 21st century after long advocacy
Sign languages: endured because communities passed them hand to hand
PLATE 63d ALT · BSL / DGS · HISTORY
